speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In this conversation with Gus Demus took place in his home in Potlatch on September 24th, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And I got home all right, but during the depression, he went down to lose the body. And after that, he started the shoeshine parlor. He was in a partnership with another fellow at that time. And when they started meeting here again, after the depression, he stayed down there anyway. He didn't come back. He was making pretty good money, you know, in a shine at that time. And after that time, you see, during the war, he had two or three girls working there for him, shining shoes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he could make as good money down there as you could make working in the mill?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, yeah. And he had a good job here in the mill. He was grading lumber, you know. He was grading different species. But he didn't come back. And Mark is a small man, you know. He can handle lumber too well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He's not too strong, you know what I mean?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That's why he stayed down there.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And there's lots of other guys that are causing him in Spokane, the Devonport Hotel, you know, his shining shoes. That's what he's doing. And he has a family, family of four boys and, you know, three boys, four boys and a girl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From his shoe shining?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He had a good job in the US. He had a story in the summer here, but he went over there and he got made. He worked for the government during the war.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In where? In Greece or?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, no, in Spokane. In Spokane, yeah. And he met a girl, Larry Mary. He was always much older than her, but they got along all right.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And he has a family, they're grown now. They're all grown, except one. He was making more money in shoeshine than to work in the mill. It must have been pretty good money in the shoeshining then, because I thought the mill was a pretty good pay. Well, yes. But in Devonport Hotel, it was better because they kept busy, you know, and he had a partner too. I mean, he hired men. And after his boys got a little older, they took their job themselves until they, while they were going to school, you know, they were shining shoes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And he did pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he leave here when the Depression hit too, your cousin?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No. He was in Spokane. But he did leave here before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In what last?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, for a long time. There were three brothers, you know. Them. One of them died here not very long ago, and the other one went to Greece and got married. And he didn't come back. And he died. He was by my age, eighty -four. He died a couple of years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did those brothers, when did the cousin of yours left?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, what I was thinking was that a lot of people seemed to leave when the Depression hit here. They left. A lot of them left, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what happened to most of the Greeks that were here?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They left in the Depression hit. The Depression spread enough. You see, they had nothing. No, nothing here. Nothing, no work of any kind.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And they never did give their Greeks a job because they worked in a mill, you see. And the mill was down. And they were given a job to some family men, maybe two or three days a week. Maybe one day a week to work in a planner, you see. They were running a planner. Part of the time. Because they were shipping lumber, you know. But they were never on the mill for two years. That was a bad Depression. And I left here, too. I went down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, yeah? What did you do down there?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, I had a friend there. He had ran a garden truck. He was raising vegetables. He had no money from the vegetables either, you know. They were making poor living. Oh, yeah, I went down there. But since they started here, I came back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you live with this guy, this friend of yours? Did you live with him, this friend of yours? Did you share a place with him in Lewiston?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, down there, yeah. Sure. I lived with him because he had a... Well, he wasn't married to the woman. He had a hard woman. And I was taking care of her. She was doing the cooking and everything. And we worked out in a field for practically nothing, just for boarding room. It was pretty tough. The Depression.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did he own his own business there? Was he just laboring for other people? The fella that I was staying with?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah. Oh, he was down there all the time. That was his business, you know. Resident... I think I got a garden truck here. He was a resident in Lewiston. And he had vegetables, carrots, and everything else.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: So he owned the food himself that he raised. Well, no. He didn't own the place he rented. I see. But he's dead now. Oh, yeah. Well, I went down there. I didn't have nothing to do here. And I had to go down there and pass the time. Because he did nothing, no place.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No job. You can't buy a job. And then, oh, after the election,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: they did some... Oh, and Rosal got elected, you see, at that time. He brought a lot of boys from the east out here for the CCC boys, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You remember that? You don't remember that?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, I heard about it, though. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what about that?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, they brought a lot of boys in and he opened the door and tried to give them a job, to give a job to everybody. I worked a couple of days a week myself in doing... working on a highway. There was nothing else, you see. And we were doing handwork in order to... in order to prolong the work. You see, there was nothing in the hurry. The reason they did, Rosal did that is to give him a job. Something to do. Give him something to eat, because people were starving at that time. It was bad.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Did you know people that were...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: didn't have enough to eat?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I was one of them myself. But I had to... well, he had enough, you know, he had to leave, but it wasn't enough to get what you want. You see, everything was cheap then, but there was no money. Ten cents for a dozen eggs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But who did you get the ten cents?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, were there days when you didn't have supper?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Once or twice, I think. Well, we had a little bit, but not much.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, it was pretty bad.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That depression, that would never come back. It would never come again unless this country could go haywire. You see, now the government takes care of the American social security, they get younger men get unemployment, and the others get these...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you call them?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Footstamps. Footstamps. And so on, you know. Everybody... And they do that a little bit too much. You see, some of the fellas say, we don't want the footsteps. They cheat, you know. Some of them are not entitled to, and they go and get them just the same. Well, Gus, I'm really curious about, you know, what it was like in a depression era. It must have felt pretty awful to wonder where your next meal was going to come from. Yeah, it was bad. And for family people, too, it was terrible. Now, here's the company, those that were married, and had families, they had to give them something to... They gave them a credit in store.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They had to give them something for the families to leave, you know. And that's why they kept them, and yeah, my job maybe once a week, or one day a week, or two, three days a week. To raise their kids in cinnamon school, too.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, that depression was terrible.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you think about going someplace else?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At that time?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, I did went to Lewiston. There was nothing here. And then pretty soon, they started the meals, you see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They gave them so much lumber to cut every year, you see, when Roger got in. He started and gave them so much. Now, I worked, I went Spokane and worked for my colleague, lumber company there, at Knight's. Because they had a quarter to cut so much lumber, you see. And they didn't cut it during the day. They had to hire Knight's shift to do it. And they could get the men any time over there. The men were plentiful in Spokane and every place else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did you stay up there? For very long?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Not very long, because they cut that quarter, or whatever it was. I don't know how many. About a month, anyway. And then they shut down. And then if they wanted more, they run two, three months, they started the meal again. You see, they had so much to cut, you see. It was set by the government.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to eat only the cheapest food that you could find?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, not all the time. Down in Lewiston, we had pretty good. Them boys in shining shoes, you know, they were making a little money. And of course, when I started to work after the Depression, I owe one fella for $400.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: $400. $400 was a lot of money that time. You see, for food.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a shoe shiner?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, he was here. But he was working and I stayed with him, you see. Because I was in the rock, I had no money. I was spending it a little bit too fast. I was getting it, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you spend it on?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Everything.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Everything. Excuse me. He said, say, you don't have to go to work for nothing anyway. But he died. He fell off the pickup truck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was the guy that lent you to $400?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah. And he left me $2 ,000 when he died. But he had a lot of money. He had $50 ,000. He made some investments. And he made some pretty good money. He made investments on that Sunshine Mining Company, you know, and paying for the dividends at that time. Now they're not paying nothing because they're not working. They've been on the strategy for a long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he had some money during the Depression?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, yeah, yeah. He had a lot of money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you do anything for him in return for the money that he gave you? Just be a good friend?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I gave him his money back $400 after I started, you know. And he even had to give up the job because he was doing the same thing down in the minute as I was doing, you see. And go to work on a section to give me a chance to go to work here, you see, because they weren't hired single men. He was working in the planet at that time. That's why they kept him here. But they didn't give him maybe one day a week or maybe one day a month sometimes because they knew he had the money, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you say he quit his job?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, he quit the mill when I started. When they started the mill, they shut the planet down and brought them, fellas, over in the mill, you know, for a while. And that didn't last very long. And, of course, he didn't take the job in the mill, but he went to work on a section. In order to give me a chance to get the job, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean you took his job?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes. He was pretty nice, fella. I'm sorry, when he died, because even near the $2 ,000 he let me. It didn't mean nothing to me because I had one of them. You see, it was after the Depression. It was in 1950. 1950 he died.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He was a good friend.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When he went to work on the section, was that still the railroad work around here?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah. There was a lot of work there, you see. In the section, for him, he was a good friend of his and he gave him a job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it pay less than what he had been making in the mill?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, yeah. He was making less, yeah. Railroad didn't have to pay really enough. They don't pay the same wages in the mill. They do now because the things change, you know. So the reason he did that was for you to have left the mill. He must have liked you. Well, he was a good friend.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he come from near where you did in Greece?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Pretty close, but not from the same town. He was all what... I didn't know him before he came here. That is, he came first. I came later. And I met him here. There was a lot of gigs, you know, them days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you pail around with him from when you first got here?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No. No, because he was without a bunch, you see. But then gradually the bunch spread out. And when the Depression came, everybody had to leave, the Greek boys, you know. Some men went to the old country, some... and got married, some went in different places, whatever they could get a job. There was nothing here for them anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the Americans who were single, that were low? They had to leave too?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes. But some, you know, they had a place around here, they had a... some branches, you know, around here, and they met Moneta. They worked on her.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Not very much. Nobody made Moneta. Not time. I was going to ask you, Gus, if you think it was harder for people like the Greeks and Italians to get ahead in the mill than it was for the Americans who had been around the country. Well, yes, it was hard. But during the war, a lot of young fellows went to the war, you know. The Greeks got changed to advanced a little bit in the mill, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: During the First World War?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: First and Second World War II. The Second World War II, you see, the Second World War. There weren't very many here, but very few.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: So the wars were really a good opportunity to get ahead for Greeks. Well, yes, for those that were here, there were not very many here then. So if there was the same... we say there was the same... there was a job open in the mill and our chance to get ahead, it might be easier for the local American to get it. Not sure. They have preference. I'm not sure that's natural, but... Oh, well, it's not natural, but they make it. They had preference before us. But I had my own job, and I didn't claim anything else because it's as high as I could go. It was... I couldn't go any higher. Which...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: this is the job as edger?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, I was a trimming loner. Trimming.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come that was as high?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, well, I started there and I quit. I didn't try to get any... I didn't try to get any other job than that. It was a good job and not very hard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Putting levers, you know, you know, you know what that is? Trimming loner?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Putting levers on the trimmer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you didn't want to try to do something else?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like the grading or that sort of thing?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, one fella, one grader asked me, he says, I want you to work for me. I says no. Because they didn't pay as much for the graders and they paid for the other, for the trimming. So there was no difference in the pay. They agreed with the mother. Now I says it's too late for me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, if you could have done anything else in the mill, is there anything you would have wanted to do besides trimming?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: There would have been a better paying job. Yeah, there was, but I didn't start there, you see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And I don't know, maybe I was a man that never progressed, you see. I was satisfied with all I had. Now and then. Well, that's probably better than wanting stuff, something that you can't get. Well, yeah. And if father's used to one kind of work, he does it easier.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And he's my first mother. Mike told me there was a foreman that he liked a lot. I can't remember the guy's name, but he had a big Greek crew. Oh, Miles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his first name?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: John. Yeah, that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember him?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, yeah. I worked for him. I worked for him for years. I worked for him from 1914 until he left.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: In, I don't know, when he left. And then, 21. No, 24, 25.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And there was no, in those days, there was no social security. You would get out of the summer and you're dead. You know, you're nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did he leave, you know?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: There was a father that got his job from Melkriva. They shot that mill down, you know. And then they brought him down here. He was a younger man. And he was crooked, too, you know, and he, he bucked him out.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, John was, didn't put you all, you know, and, and they didn't retire them in those days. They let him work as long as they could work. There was no retirement.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he stick around the mill or did he, did he just leave?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He left. He went, he went to work out there up in Canada for a while and then he came back to Spokane. And he stayed in Spokane. I think he bought a house in Spokane and he stayed in Spokane. He had no money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his, his job? Was he the foreman for the whole mill?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah. He was former for the saw mill and then another foreman in the planner, you know, in the shipping department. They have a lot of foreman's. They did have them, but I don't know how.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was, what was Myers like to, to the men? What was he like to work for? What did you think of him as a foreman?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah. He was a good man. He was very good man. He was honest. He was true. He tried to do the best. If you are good, if you are a good man, you are doing your work and, and he changed him up for you to advance. He gave it to you. He wanted, whether you are Greek or Italian or any nationality.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He was honest man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean he didn't give preference to the local boys?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No. Not if they didn't have it. They didn't earn it. He was alright. But you were saying before that the local boys did get easier. Yes, some of them, yeah, but not all of them. They probably had it coming. Oh, because they had been around longer. When John was here, no, he not only department what they are doing, I don't know.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: There was a fellow by the name of Charlie.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He was super thin.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Charlie is somebody I can't remember his last name.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: So you were talking Mike down there. Yeah. Well, wait a minute.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who is this Charlie guy? He wasn't so good? Who? Was he crooked?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, he run the whole business. He was all country sweet. Oh,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: it doesn't matter his last name. I know, his last name too. I just wondered why you mentioned him. Oh, well he was a super thin on the planner.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, I talked to Mike and he told me that most of the Greeks worked in the same. One by one, they got a job over there and they didn't know anything else to do. If they were not, they probably used semi -scale work, you see, and they couldn't do it. Most of them started in the middle, and they were a lot of common labor work. And there were very few, not very many, maybe two or three fellows that worked outside. But most of them worked in the middle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And most of them started as common labor in the middle?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, yes. I mean, they didn't start like a Sawyer or an engineer. No, it's a Sawyer and engineer. They got to that later, but it's a skilled job, you see, and they expect you to do the work. And if you don't know, you don't know the tech to be Sawyer because it's a responsibility there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the types of jobs that the guys started at in the middle?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: There were a lot of kinds of jobs at that time, but now them jobs are all gone. The machinery took the place of the jobs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But in them days, what were the jobs?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, I can't explain it to you because there are so many jobs that they do well with them. They had a job, 10 Sawyer. They called him 10 Sawyer. He was behind the Sawyer and he put him on the porch when they come from the job. When the Sawyer cut them, you see, so they wanted to take up and get the ball up there. And they needed to have that. They had a service that they were sent in the carriage. Now they don't have any number in the carriage. Only one in the Sawyer, the Sawyer from the carriage and casket lumber. It's one man where there were three, you see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It's quite a difference, lots of difference. There are lots of men working on that. That's a terrible plot.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Lots of men working over there. Cleaning up.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Maybe two, three men, four, five men in a minute that they clean up. And if somebody wanted to go out for,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: you know, to take a leak or to go to toilet or something like that, they had a man, an extra man there. And now they only got one. And he's busy too. They don't fool around with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the first job that you took in the middle?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I was a slasher. You see, I was a... And I didn't even know what that was, you know, when he told me. He said, you got the slasher. Well, John. And he showed you a point, you know, that way. Well, I figured that... And one Greek was working there. And he motioned me, come on. What we were doing there was straightening these slabs from the logs. They were dropping on the floor, you know. They had a long strand and they came to a source. And they were cut to four feet for lathe. They were making lathe in days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what the lathe is?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Now they did well with that for, oh, I don't know how many years. And that's my first job. I had to pull straight with the saw, you see, to trim it on one end. And then cut four foot piece for a lathe start. But that wasn't too skilled a start. Not skilled a job, no. But they were paying log ledges. That was two dollars an hour. I mean two dollars a day for ten hours. You know, one thing that Mike said to me was that mostly the Greeks were in the saw mill and mostly the Italians were working in the green chain and the yards. And I was wondering why it worked that way, that the Greeks were inside and the Italians outside. Well, the Greeks maybe were smart. They got in their garage probably. And they stayed there, you see. And the Italians, they were working in the green chain. I think they were paying a little bit more to now out in the yard they were contracting. They were... GIPO. They were GIPOing and they were making a little more money. They were not only Italians but Swiss. All Swiss were yard men, you see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They GIPOed Muslim. And there were other jobs around the stockers and all over the place.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But the Greeks, Muslim was in the saw mill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And any of the Greeks that you knew and any of them worked in the saw mill back in Greece?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No saw mills.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No saw mills there at all, eh?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Not in the other part, not in the southern part of the world where I came from. Maybe very few in the extreme north up in Macedonia or the... Or they have timber but not very much. There's no saw mills. Most of the timber over there is coming from Austria and... Austria was the most. That they were sending us closer to Greece, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think it was better to be in the saw mill than working in the saw mill than other places in the operation?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, I was... It was better because you were in dark, you see. No rain, no snow, no nothing. You could work here around without getting wet. And in the green chance it was pretty cold in the days.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: We had to wear a Mackinac horse in the saw mill sometime. Only it was cold. And that's why the Greeks stayed there because they didn't want to get out in the cold weather. Even if they had made a little more last morning. It sounds like maybe there was a better chance to advance inside the mill, too. I mean, there was more jobs. Well, it was that they advanced. There wasn't very many. There was a lot of agilement and saw years, that's all. They had four agilement and four saw years. And the cedars, of course, they didn't want to money for it. Because they logged the saw, you see, gradually. And if the saw was quit by the one where the cedars take its place.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: There's only four of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So most of the jobs inside the saw mill were unskilled or they weren't highly skilled like?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No. No, they were semi -skilled, they called them. So you had to know a little bit about it. Yeah.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And after the union came in here, they classified them as skilled or semi -skilled or whatever, or common number. Or something like that, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember when the AFL started in here, when they started the union?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, shoot. I joined the AFL. And I stayed as long as they were here, but they had all the trouble in the days. And I guess the CIO, I mean...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: CIO?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: CIO. They went there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The election?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, they had the election. And they were in the structure over here, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The CIO did?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But they lost me, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of trouble, when you say trouble, what kind of trouble did they have in those days?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, the trouble was that the some of the CIOs wanted to join the union, so they could get all together, you see, and go and start. But it didn't work. But the CIO won the election, and they had the agency, you know. They had the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The local elite?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah. They had the agency there, and they could do anything they wanted. Although they couldn't force you to join the union at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think the CIO got more for the men than the AFL?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, no, they are both. The same. But I mean in those days. The same. Well, yeah, the CIO was doing more radical. You know, it was a little bit... They were asking too much.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But now they are just both. Same thing. Same thing. Well, this...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Myers... Mike was telling me something about him, some stories about him, yeah. Never find anybody. But he reprimanded him. And sometimes he lay him off. It would be 20 days or a week. The point is he's done with you. So he was alright.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the men spend any time with him outside of work? Off of work?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No. He was a... He was divorced. He didn't have no wife. He was... Well, not better, but... Divorged his wife back east, on a comm He was living alone. Him and my father lived together for a while. And then the other fella quit, I guess, or something, and he lived alone. He was born in some woman over there. German.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he spend most of his time with management? Myers?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, he spent his time reading at home. And when Mire was running nights, he had something to do with that too, you see. He had to see that the night shift was running too.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Another thing that Mike told me was that once some of the guys tried to kick some of the Greeks out of the sawmill and get the jobs. I think this was maybe in some hard times. And he said that Laird defended the Greeks and said they'd been here for a longer time. They got the jobs, they earned them, and they're going to keep them. Oh, yeah. They were honest. As long as you can do the work, they kept you. Laird was the general manager. He don't know the works, you know. Everything, not the company.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It isn't like now. Well, you figured that once the Greeks started getting in the sawmill, then they just kept on getting in after they got a start in there. And that's how they got so many in the sawmill. Yeah. As to say, instead of working outside on the green chain. Yeah, that's right. They just happened to get in there, I don't know how. From the beginning, I guess, they started in the mill and then they, one by one. And there were some that worked outside, but not very many.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Not very many.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You know, in the formation of the group. That's very few. You know, I had heard that you had a brother here. Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to him?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He died.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: His dad is my tailor. But Mike said something that he went haywire. Something happened to him. But he didn't tell me what. Oh, he was sick.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He was in medical late. He died.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Even now he's been, as he said.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And I had to really say he softened over. I see what they call that kind of mental disease.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It's been a long time, and he's been there for a long time.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Paranoia. Paranoia.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what that is?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You get fearful of everything around you. When you're fearful and you hear noises, and that is dangerous, I took him all over. I took him to Portland. I went to Portland one time. And one doctor there told me a few of my twin brothers told me to put him in the asylum. And I didn't do it then, but then leaving at worse, you know. And he was dangerous, you know, because he was under the influence of them noises. And you see, he's like the kid, somebody. So I told him in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he trust you? Your brother?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, yeah. Well, he has to go before George, you know. We took him over in Spokane. He was in Spokane. And he lived in Spokane for years. He's got to live for one year in order to get to go in Spokane. Asylum, you know. Otherwise, I don't know what would happen.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: We took him to George. And George asked him only one question.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He says, yeah, you hear voices, this?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He says yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Who is talking to you?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He says the degenerates. And that's all. The degenerates. The degenerates. You know, people that are...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You're seeing things, you know, that are not real.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, this is the same as anything else. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any idea how he got it? How it started?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No idea. We started gradually, apparently. I don't know. I don't know how he started. But then he got to be... It was and was and was until I was going to send him back to Germany. But they have no facilities. So there was one doctor in Poland. He told me there is no other place like the United States except in Germany. They have a few places like that where they keep him. But in the other places, other countries are...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he was getting so he was dangerous to other people?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes. There's noises, you know. And you're seeing...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Directly, you might tell him to go and kill so and so how. It killed me. He did that one time. He got a knife, but he didn't use it. You can put your knife in the kitchen. I was staying with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he take care of him?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No. He would take care of himself. But he was in... ...once or sometime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he threatened you with the knife?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Once, once, yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I was in the situation where I said I better take him in because he might kill somebody else. He might kill himself. And over there he's safe anyway. In the medical age, he died there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he live for long after he went in?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He lived for quite a while. I would travel times to see him, but I didn't. I couldn't hear nothing out of him.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And he didn't want me to... He didn't want me to go. He would let me alone, he says.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That was odd, but... Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to anybody?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, they say that ten percent of the people in the country have a mental... Mental effect some kind. Ten percent, that's a lot of people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he keep working in the mill as this went on? As he got worse?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No. You're saying it again.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I had to send him money, you know, or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he want?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That's why he was waiting to get in there. Or this was before... I said, well, I couldn't... You know, it cost money. And to say he's broken, and the thing is that when he was hanging around there, he was fighting with everybody. In a big place there, you know, a big restaurant where he was eating. And he was from my hometown, one of the one that owned the place. And he wrote me and told me, I better come over and take care of him, because he's fighting with everybody. And he couldn't kick him out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He couldn't kick him out because he didn't want to?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, he didn't want to, no. He didn't want to do that. Because he knew me, you see. Yeah, and he came from some corner, from some village, from the rest together. And that's why they're dead now, or something. Well, was this while...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this during the time that he was waiting before he could get into Medical Lake? Or was this before you decided to put him away?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I decided because he wrote and told me that he's fighting with everybody. That goes there, you see. And he says he must do something.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: So I stayed in this situation, and it was expensive, too. It was a good place for him anyway. There's no other place. Then the Medical Lake had a pretty good place. They take good care of him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that's when you took him to the judge?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes. Well, before you take him to that asylum, the judge has to examine him. The judge has to only win in jail, and from there they take him in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he had to live there for a year in Washington first?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes. Before he could go. Before he would go to Washington, yeah. Otherwise we would have to go to Idaho. But it's a better place over there. Oh, he lived a few years there. I don't know, not very many.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he older than you or younger?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He had a stiff leg, you know. When he was young, he had what they call osteomyogit He had the one leg was shorter than the other, and he had a pain when he was young. He suffered terribly. It was a mistake anyway. The old man should bury him over in a fresh place.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But he's well now, for him.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He shouldn't have brought him over because he would have a harder time in this country. Well, he wasn't strong enough to work. In any job, you see, it's a job that you can't do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, work down here, all right?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He worked. He had a lot. But he couldn't get along with his helpers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you finally quit?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Quit on the fighting?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I don't know. I don't remember. It's been a long time. Mike said that he and you both were easier about money, spending money and enjoying it, having a good time. And a lot of guys were. Some guys would try to hold on to every cent they made, but you and him, both were guys that lived pretty good when you could. Oh, well, not much difference. Some had to spend money.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I'd say more than go back to Greece, but some did, and some did.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It's a matter of, oh, I spent a lot of money and I went to Greece and spent it, and I came back. I'm not sorry, though. I went to see how the country was after so many years. After 53 years. I went over there, and saw people that I left six, seven years old. They were old. All men, now. That's ours. My sister, I left her. She was going to school, and we were going to school over there. And Tika was going to heck. She didn't have no teeth, nothing.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It's terrible. Terrible leaving over there. So I stayed five months. I says, goodbye. I think you mentioned to me that your relatives wanted some money that you had, too. Oh, they wanted. They got some, but not all of it. They were in a bad shape. You had to help them. My sister was there. She was a widow woman with two kids, and the husband was no good when he was leaving. They spent money when he was in the hospital. They were broke. Everything was on the,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: what you call, they were going to sell money. They were going to... They dropped. Oh, it must. Yeah.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, it must have been. Hopefully you had to have them. I couldn't have. You know.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they figure that you were rich because you were American?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, they know that I was rich. I knew that I had little money. I didn't tell them. But people around there, you know, they talk.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, yeah. Every day, you know, I mean, they go there very often. They're cost a million. Whatever they want to try, they go there. They don't try to know their own truth. But most of them do. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were back there, Gus in Greece, did you go to any, did you get a chance to see any of the old kind of customs, the old, like, the celebrations that they used to have? Did they still have that, like, Easter and holiday?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I happened to be there at the Easter, yeah. No, they have. But they don't have, like, they used to have. Not as elaborate, you know. They kind of didn't wear. I heard they were very colorful. Yeah, they were. They were colorful. They had lamps in the speed, you know, and everything else. And they do it now, but they don't have as much. You see,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: they're celebrate. They, I don't know, they got a little money.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They seem to travel a lot. They got a good, you see, all the same, being a highway's over there, you know, and they got good transportation.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They travel from one place to the other. And before they didn't have that. Now they go to Athens pretty near every week. And some, they, they want anything, like, and some they travel with the boat, some with the bus, and they got two, two, or two wheels.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And if you go with the boat, and you get sick, why are you having luck?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I did that my niece, you know, the girl, she got sick, she got sick and a dog. She was vomiting. I told her father to take a dog. They got a place down below there where they got bumps, you know, bats and put him down to sleep.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But you have to pay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made you decide to come back? To, to, what made you decide to come back to America instead of, instead of stay over there? What made you decide to come back here instead of stay over in Greece?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, maybe. Yeah. I didn't, I didn't understand any good, just to stay in the first place. I went over there to visit. Yeah, I didn't know. I won't say that. I didn't have nothing here.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And here I didn't have nothing here. There didn't help me anything, but it's very easy to live.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: What the heck?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: If I was there, I would probably be in debt today. I'm putting you to be in debt here. Now, this thing. Well, you know, Gus, with so many people, so many people going back to Europe from this country in those days, so many of them, it seems like so many went back.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Was there, what was your thinking about that?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Did you, I'm talking about, say, in the 20s and the 30s. You never did go back.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You wanted to stay here, didn't you?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, yeah, sure. I never, what made me go back mostly, I joined Masonic large here a long time ago, 30 years ago.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And I was a grand representative for Greece. And I thought I'd take a trip and see them and see my sister and the other relationship. I got a lot of cousins. I was, like, the fellows at work, you know, went back there and got married. And I enjoyed the trip, but I spent a lot of money because my nearest folks, my sister, was in bad shape and I had to help her. I couldn't get away. That's the reason I spent money. And now they sold everything from there. I had to give them power of attorney to sell them. And I didn't ask for money or anything. They won't send anything anyway. And they moved about ten miles from where, close to sea. And my niece, she got married there to a boy.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And I don't write to them now and they don't write to me because I got blind and I can't write anyway.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: So...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, back in the 20s, when so many people came over here for a while and made some money and then went back to the old country, did you think about going back to the old country or did you really always want to stay here?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I never thought I had no... I never had no ambition, no... no... never did want to go back and stay.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Because this is the best one in the world to live in. I don't know what to say and I saw. And I knew it in a frying... before I went, but I didn't know exactly.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: We know...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: all these other guys, why did they go back?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Your cousins that worked here. Well, two of them got married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You see?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And they bought a piece of land and they wanted to get married there and they were supposed to have something, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, the dowry?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, they got a dowry. And they thought that they could make a living without. But I never thought of that. I never expected that or I never thought anything of that kind. I thought this was good country to live in, and I said, and I was going to stay with it.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: This is a sport called the... those that have money and they live a very good life in big cities or they have convenience but in rural districts like my hometown. They don't have nothing. They don't have no electricity. They don't have a done thing. They cook in a... It's summertime where they let the fire go out, but they have to build a fire in order to cook. They don't have no songs or anything. Some have, not have money. But if they don't have the money, it's nothing. Stay away.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: In the wintertime, it's cold over there too. Sometime I get up, four o 'clock in the morning to take the bus. I told my sister, I'm going to Athens. I rented a house over there in Athens anyway and I just went to visit.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It cost me a lot of money but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you decided to, at four o 'clock in the morning, you decided to leave?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, it's pretty cold. There's no fire. That little fire didn't give no heat in the fireplace.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And I said goodbye. We're going to Athens. Athens is... They have everything there, but it cost money. It cost just as much. It cost two dollars a day for coal oil to burn the soil. Oh, that's too much. Yeah, it's pretty high. Pretty high, the oil and coal oil and the gas. Gas is terribly high. I don't know what gas was, but it was up to a dollar, a dollar and a half. A gallon then. Now it's maybe two, three dollars. I don't know. They don't have any. They use coal oil. They have changed their engines in the automobiles to burn coal oil inside the gas. And all them taxi drivers and things, you know, they all got set for a different kind of oil oil.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how were your cousins doing that had worked here and gone back? How were they?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They're doing that. They are dead now. One of them did pretty good. He owned our land and he was pretty good. But the others, they were getting long. Not very much, but now I heard that they were both dead. And I got two years.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: There's no living over there.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And for men of age, you know, I was 70 years old when I went over there. This, I didn't think, was a place for me to stay.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And if I wanted to get married, I can get married here anytime. Now I can get married because I'm blind and I'm a handicap and I'm sick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You want anything?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, I'm going to have to get going. I'll get going because I got to do, I just figured I'd stop by for a little while. I wasn't even going to start asking you questions and stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you take a record?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, some of it. Most of it, not all of it. You know, one other thing that I was going to ask you, because it was about this business of being a bachelor, say back in the 20s, you know, the teens in the 20s and the 30s.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much chance for all these bachelors like yourself and other guys to get married? To get married?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, well, yeah, there was. Some of them got married, but not all. It wasn't much chance as it is now because we were living together and we never mixed with people at the town here where they get married. Boys. And we couldn't speak the English language. That's not a thing, you see, that kept us from getting married.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And now that we learn, we get to all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But there was no, say like, there weren't any Greek girls around, were there?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No big girls, no. We got one big girl here.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But she came when she was ten years old, and she's married to some young fella. Some young fella here.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That's a good girl. Her father was businessman in Chicago, and she came to San Francisco, and she met this, first of all, from Gakil in the war, and she met this boy from here. And followed by the name of Arson, and she's been here for ten years now or more. More than ten years.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But she's not here in Padmas. They started here and they didn't like it. And then they saw that there was much change for events. And they went down to Queens.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Queens in Washington. That's where they got the big other one. Two, three farms over there. They got a lot of money. And she made, she got two, three kids. One kid is, with her first girlfriend, one girl, she made, and the other they got two more, I think. One is twenty years old, and the other one, I don't know why he's still young.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These guys that went back to Greece to get married, did any of them, many of them bring their wives back over here?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Not very many. I wonder why they didn't come back with their wives. Well, as I said before, they got dowry, you see. They got a piece of land there, and they started to walk over there to raise something to eat and live there.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That's why.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And they didn't speak very good English, you know, and they said, oh, what the heck, we don't go back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who uses?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, that's the, that's the, the mic to go with that thing. Oh.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, it does seem, it does seem to me that a little too bad that there wasn't more chance for the, for the bachelors to, you know, to meet the American girls around here. Well, there was a chance, a strong chance, but they didn't, they didn't trust them. They didn't trust the American girls, those boys that had a little money, you know. They said, maybe I marry her and she will take my money or spend my money and then kick me out. You see, that's not a thing. Although I never thought that way because I didn't, I didn't have my money in the first place. And when I did have money, it was too late. I was too old.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I tried to marry one at a time, but she didn't, when she was working, one townside. I didn't have it. And since then, it's my next door number now and she's a widow.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: This girl was, she wasn't Greek though. No, no, from you. She was from Balac. She's the one who passed, no.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, I sent them records all back. They sent me cards, you know, about them records that I had there. Yeah, yeah.
